elements.sent-2411-001: Combining classical density functional theory (cDFT) with quantum mechanics (QM) methods offers a computationally efficient alternative to traditional QM/molecular mechanics (MM) approaches for modeling mixed quantum-classical systems at finite temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, both QM/MM and QM/cDFT rely on approximations that introduce ambiguities.
elements.sent-2411-003: This series of papers aims to establish a rigorous theoretical framework to guide the development of a QM/cDFT formulation that clarifies the approximations involved.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this first paper, we establish a comprehensive density functional theory (DFT) framework for mixed quantum (QM)-classical (MM) systems within the canonical ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-005: We derive the equilibrium density matrix for a system comprising $\nqm$ QM and $\nmm$ MM particles and, based on this derivation, propose a variational formulation of the Helmholtz free energy in terms of the full QM-MM density matrix.
elements.sent-2411-006: By leveraging permutational symmetry and thanks to constrained-search methods, we reformulate the variational problem using functionals of objects having reduced dimensionalities.
elements.sent-2411-007: Three different formulations are presented, each using quantities with progressively lower dimensionalities.
elements.sent-2411-008: This ultimately enables computation of the Helmholtz free energy using only the QM and MM one-body densities, generalizing both cDFT and electronic DFT (eDFT) for QM-MM systems.
elements.sent-2411-009: In a subsequent paper, this formalism will be extended to the grand canonical ensemble, allowing classical particle number fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-010: This extension, in which cDFT is most naturally formulated, provides a framework for studying solvation problems.
elements.sent-2411-011: The Hohenberg-Kohn (HK) theorems\cite{HohKoh-PR-64 dramatically reduce the complexity of simulating a quantum ($\qmb$) molecular system by enabling a focus on the one-electron density instead of the full $N$-electron wave function.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Central to this simplification is the unknown universal density functional, which was later given a more rigorous mathematical foundation by Levy and Lieb\cite{Levy-PNAS-79,Lieb-IJQC-83 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Through the self-consistent framework introduced by Kohn and Sham\cite{KohSha-PR-65 , along with extensive efforts to develop approximations to the exact exchange-correlation functional (see, for example, a recent review in Ref. \onlinecite{Toulouse-DFT-23 ), electronic density functional theory (eDFT) has become the workhorse of quantum chemistry.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Although electronic structure calculations at zero temperature can routinely handle systems with hundreds to even thousands of atoms, accounting for finite-temperature effects becomes crucial in many applications.
elements.sent-2411-015: Such calculations must therefore be set in a statistical ensemble, where the leading-order entropic contribution arises from nuclear motion.
elements.sent-2411-016: In this context, it is natural to compute the forces acting on the nuclei at the quantum level and then to solve the classical equations of motion to propagate the trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-017: This forms the basis of ab-initio molecular dynamics (AIMD)\cite{iftimie_ab_2005,silpar-prl-99 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Despite AIMD's considerable achievements, it is computationally demanding due to the extensive number of $\qmb$ calculations required to sample the exponentially large phase space.
elements.sent-2411-019: The convergence rate of the statistics can also vary significantly depending on the targeted properties and conditions\cite{PanSpaHarSceGal-PNAS-13,RozPanGibGal-PNAS-18 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: To alleviate AIMD's computational burden, $\qmb$/$\mmb$ methods were developed, where only a small, critical portion of the system is treated at the $\qmb$ level, while the remainder is handled at the classical level.
elements.sent-2411-021: The interaction between the classical particles are modeled using parametrized force fields and their dynamics obey Newton's law of motion.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, even in $\qmb$/$\mmb$ approaches, sampling the phase space of large systems remains computationally intensive.
elements.sent-2411-023: Inspired by the extension of eDFT to $\qmb$ systems at finite temperatures\cite{Mermin-PR-65 , density-based formalisms have emerged for describing purely classical objects in statistical mechanics, particularly in the form of classical density functional theory (cDFT)\cite{Evans-AP-79 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Originally rooted in liquid state theory (LST), cDFT is essentially a classical counterpart to eDFT, reformulating the statistical mechanics problem of large ensembles of identical particles (atoms or molecules) in terms of one-body density functionals.
elements.sent-2411-025: At this minimum, the functional is equal to the grand potential, which serves as the thermodynamic potential of the GC ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-026: Therefore, most equilibrium properties can be obtained through functional minimization over the classical one-body density rather than through tedious sampling of configurations in an exponentially large phase space.
elements.sent-2411-027: The efficiency of both eDFT and cDFT stems from a common principle: each method replaces the challenge of computing a high-dimensional object—such as the wavefunction in QM or the probability distribution in MM—with the optimization of a functional that depends only on a low-dimensional quantity, i.e. the one-body density of either quantum electrons or classical molecules.
elements.sent-2411-028: The mathematical framework allowing for such a simplification is the so-called constrained-search principle introduced by Levy and Lieb\cite{Levy-PNAS-79,Lieb-IJQC-83 which enables, under certain constraints of representability, to give an explicit expression of the universal functionals of eDFT and cDFT, although not usable in practical calculations.}
elements.sent-2411-029: In this approach, the total Hamiltonian is partitioned into well defined QM and MM Hamiltonians, while the QM/MM interaction lacks of a unique definition as usual in QM/MM approaches.
elements.sent-2411-030: The QM/MM coupling is usually described as the sum of an electrostatic interaction combined with a non-bonded term which is often modeled using Lennard-Jones force field to account for repulsion and dispersion effects\cite{senn_qmmm_2009,giovannini_general_2017,clemente_best_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: QM/cDFT advantageously replaces the exhaustive phase-space sampling required in QM/MM by a self-consistent optimization of both classical and quantum components.
elements.sent-2411-032: While QM/cDFT approaches offer potentially an attractive computational scaling, they do not clearly emerge as approximations of a general, exact theory-which remains to be developed.
elements.sent-2411-033: This work aims to address this gap by developing an exact mathematical framework that enables a general mixed QM/MM system to be described in terms of reduced quantities, ultimately yielding a rigorous DFT equivalent for such systems.
elements.sent-2411-034: Here, we focus on the canonical ensemble, deriving equations to compute the Helmholtz free energy using only the one-body classical density and either the $N$-electron density or the one-electron density.
elements.sent-2411-035: The extension to the grand canonical ensemble, along with applications of this QM/MM formalism, will be presented in the second paper of this series.
elements.sent-2411-036: A foundational step in developing this DFT formulation is establishing the equilibrium density (ED) in the canonical ensemble for a mixed QM/MM system, achieved here through a Wigner transformation and a semiclassical expansion.
elements.sent-2411-037: Then, applying the Levy-Lieb constrained search and assuming additive two-body interactions, we naturally express the free energy in terms of reduced quantities, such as the one-electron density and the classical one-body density.
elements.sent-2411-038: Practical approximations and connections to previously proposed methods will also be discussed in the second part of this series.

elements.sent-2411-043: The main goal of this section is to give a variational formulation for the Helmholtz free energy of a mixed $\qmb$/$\mmb$ system based on objects involving $\nqm$ and $\nmm$ variables.

elements.subsection-2411-001: Recall of the variational formulation of statistical quantum mechanics in the canonical ensemble
elements.sent-2411-047: Let us consider a $\qmb$ system composed of $N$ particles, not necessarily identical, described by the Hamiltonian $\fulhn$.
elements.sent-2411-048: In the canonical ensemble, The ED matrix $\rdmneq$ has the following expression \begin{equation}\label{eq:def_ed_cano} \rdmneq = \frac{e^{-\beta \fulhn}}{\zfunc},\end{equation} with $\beta^{-1 = k_B T$ and $\zfunc$ is the partition function. \begin{equation}\zfunc = \trn\{ e^{-\beta \fulhn} \},\end{equation} where we introduced the trace of an $N$-electron operator, $\hat{O ^N$, which is given in real space by \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \trn\{ \hat{O}^N \} = \int \text{d}q_1 \hdots \text{d}q_{N} \elemm{\rlots{N}}{\hat{O}}{\rlots{N}} = \int \text{d}q_1 \hdots \text{d}q_{N} \oprr. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The corresponding Helmholtz free energy is then obtained by \begin{equation}\label{eq:def_pot_cano} \potcanoz = - \frac{1}{\beta} \log(\zfunc).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Any other thermodynamic equilibrium properties can be obtained from the knowledge of $\rdmneq$ using ensemble average.
elements.sent-2411-050: Although those well established results can be derived directly by arguments inherent to statistical mechanics, they can also be obtained through a variational formulation of statistical mechanics, which was first proposed by Gibbs in the classical case and latter on extended to quantum mechanics by Von Neumann\cite{Neumann-Pri-96 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: In the latter framework, $\potcanoz$ can be expressed as the minimum of a free-energy functional \begin{equation}\label{eq:pot_cano_1} \potcanoz = \min_{\rdmn} \potcano[\rdmn],\end{equation} where $\rdmn$ are $N$-representable density matrices, i.e. density matrices representing statistical mixing of $N$-particles density matrices.
elements.sent-2411-052: The density matrix minimizing Eq.\eqref{eq:pot_cano_1 is then the ED matrix $\rdmneq$ of Eq. \eqref{eq:def_ed_cano , and the functional of Eq. \eqref{def:potcano evaluated at $\rdmneq$ is the Helmholtz free energy $\potcanoz$ of Eq. \eqref{eq:def_pot_cano .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Two special cases of this variational framework are worth mentioning in the present context.
elements.sent-2411-054: Firstly, at zero temperature, the entropic term vanishes and the corresponding ED matrix is nothing but $\rdmneq = \ket{\Psi_0}\bra{\Psi_0 $, where $\ket{\Psi_0}$ is the ground state of $\fulhn$.}
elements.sent-2411-055: This is then equivalent to the variational principle routinely used in quantum chemistry, but here it is formalized in terms of $N$-body density matrices rather than $N$-body wave functions.
elements.sent-2411-056: Secondly, in the classical case, the density matrices $\rdmn$ reduce to probability distributions in terms of momentum/position variables, and the Hamiltonian operator becomes the classical Hamiltonian function.
elements.sent-2411-057: The latter can be obtained from first principle through an expansion in powers of $\hbar$ of the Wigner transformation of the fully quantum system, and then keeping the zeroth-order term, as shown by Wigner in his seminal work on semi-classical expansion\cite{Wigner-PR-32 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: The variational formulation reduces then to that proposed by Gibbs, i.e. the Helmholtz free energy is the minimum of the classical free energy functional analog of Eq. \eqref{def:potcano , that is reached for the equilibrium probability distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-059: The advantage of a variational framework is that one can apply all the tools from functional analysis to reformulate this cumbersome problem in simpler ways.
elements.sent-2411-060: Also, alternative formulations can be obtained by reducing the dimensionality of the functions on which one performs the minimization.
elements.sent-2411-061: Striking examples of the advantage of the latter are certainly the universal Levy-Lieb functional of the electron density of eDFT at zero temperature, or the formulation of the classical statistical problems of cDFT.
elements.sent-2411-062: In what follows, we wish to provide a variational formulation of statistical mechanics for a mixed quantum-classical system.
elements.subsection-2411-002: The Hamiltonian of the quantum-classical system
elements.sent-2411-063: We wish to generalize the variational formulation of statistical quantum mechanics recalled above to a mixed $\qmb$/$\mmb$ system.
elements.sent-2411-064: We start by the definition of the Hamiltonian describing the system considered here.
elements.sent-2411-065: We adopt the following notation conventions : i) lowercase letters will refer to purely quantum objects (both variables and operators) while capital letters will refer to either purely classical or mixed quantum-classical objects and ii) all bold letters refer to collective variables or operators (i.e. relative to all quantum or classical particles) and non bold letters will refer to individual or pairwise variables or operators.

elements.sent-2411-141: The last and simplest functional depends on two separated one-body classical and quantum densities (see Eq. \eqref{eq:pot_cano_11 ).}
elements.sent-2411-142: These successive formulations allow to give a rigorous mathematical bridge between eDFT and cDFT for $\qmb$/$\mmb$ systems.
elements.sent-2411-143: The second opus of this paper will be dedicated to the application of the present formalism to the case of molecular $\qmb$/$\mmb$ simulations.
elements.subsection-2411-008: A more symmetric expression for the equilibrium density
elements.sent-2411-144: Using the Zassenhaus formula the equilibrium density of Eq. \eqref{eq:eq_qmmm_rho can be rewritten \begin{equation}\label{eq:eq_qmmm_rho_bis} \begin{aligned} \eqrdm(\boldqb,\boldqs,\boldqps) = \frac{1}{\eqz} \elemm{\boldqs}{e^{-\beta\hathqm} \hat{\mathcal{C}}(\boldqb)}{\boldqps} e^{-\beta\big(\vmm(\boldqb)+\boldwqmmm(\boldqb)\big)}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where now appears an operator $\hat{\mathcal{C (\boldqb)$ involving an infinite product of exponential of operators \begin{equation}\hat{\mathcal{C}}(\boldqb)) = e^{\hat{C}_1} e^{\hat{C}_2} \hdots ,\end{equation} where the operator $\hat{C _n$ involves intricated $n$-th order nested commutators between $\hatboldts$ and $\hatboldwqmmm(\boldqb)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-145: For instance, up to second order, the operators $C_1$ and $C_2$ have the following expression \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} C_1 = +\frac{\beta^2}{2} \big[ \hatboldts,\hatboldwqmmm(\boldqb) \big], C_2 = -\frac{\beta^3}{3} \big(\big[\hatboldwqmmm(\boldqb),\big[ \hatboldts,\hatboldwqmmm(\boldqb) \big]\big] +\frac{1}{2} \big[\hatboldts,\big[ \hatboldts,\hatboldwqmmm(\boldqb) \big]\big]\big). \end{aligned}\end{equation} Unfortunately, this expansion does not truncate at finite order as it involves a priori an arbitrary high order of nested commutators between $\hatboldts$ and $\big[\hatboldts,\hatboldwqmmm(\boldqb)\big] $ which never vanishes unless the function $\boldwqmmm(\boldqb)$ has a finite Taylor expansion (i.e. at some point the derivative of $\boldwqmmm(\boldqb)$ with respect to $\boldqs$ vanishes).
elements.sent-2411-146: Therefore, although the expression in Eq. \eqref{eq:eq_qmmm_rho_bis is more symmetrical than that of Eq. \eqref{eq:eq_qmmm_rho , it is of no practical use.}}
elements.subsection-2411-009: Derivation of the ED for a $\qmb$/$\mmb$ system
elements.sent-2411-147: In this section, we report the derivations leading to the ED for a mixed $\qmb$/$\mmb$ system (see Eqs.
elements.sent-2411-148: The big picture is the following: we start from a fully quantum system containing $\nqm+\nmm$ particles, and we consider the ED in the canonical ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-149: Then, we make a semi-classical expansion of the Wigner transformation of the ED, where only the $\nmm$ variables corresponding to the heavy particles have been transformed.
elements.sent-2411-150: Eventually, we make an expansion both in terms of the ratio of the masses $m/M$ and $\hbar$.
elements.sent-2411-153: As our goal is to eventually obtain $\nmm$ purely classical particles which are naturally described in phase space, we will first exchange the couple of real-space variables $(\boldqb,\boldqpb)$ by a couple of phase-space variables $(\boldqb,\boldpb)$ in Eq. \eqref{eq:eqrdm_real , which can be done by a Wigner transformation\cite{Wigner-PR-32 .}}
elements.sent-2411-154: A crucial property of the Wigner transformation is that it allows to obtain traces of quantum operators as classical traces in phase space (for a pedagogical introduction to Wigner transform, the reader might refer to Ref. \onlinecite{Case-AJP-08 ).}
elements.sent-2411-155: More precisely, if $\hat{A}$, $\hat{B}$ are two quantum operators, and $\tilde{A _W(q,p)$, $\tilde{B _W(q,p)$ their respective Wigner transformation, then the following property holds \begin{equation}\tr\{ \hat{A}\hat{B} \} = \int \text{d}q\text{d}p\tilde{A}_W(q,p)\tilde{B}_W(q,p).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-156: In our case, the Wigner transformation of $\hateqrdm$ is performed only on the $\nmm$ variables, which, by definition, leads to \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \label{eq:def_wigner} \tilde{W}_0(\boldqb,\boldqs;\boldpb,\boldqps) = \int d\boldqpb e^{i\boldpb \cdot \boldqpb/\hbar} \eqrdm(\boldqb+\frac{\boldqpb}{2},\boldqs;\boldqb-\frac{\boldqpb}{2},\boldqps) = \int d\boldqpb e^{i\boldpb \cdot \boldqpb/\hbar} \elemm{\boldqb+\frac{\boldqpb}{2},\boldqs}{\hateqrdm}{\boldqb-\frac{\boldqpb}{2},\boldqps} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where here we emphasize that $\boldqb$, $\boldqpb$ and $\boldpb$ are scalars and not operators.
elements.sent-2411-157: We will now carry some calculations similar to Eqs.
elements.sent-2411-158: One can rewrite Eq.\eqref{eq:def_wigner as \begin{equation}\label{eq:def_wigner_2} \begin{aligned} \tilde{W}_0(\boldqb,\boldqs;\boldpb,\boldqps) = \int d\boldqpb e^{i\boldpb \cdot (\boldqb+\frac{\boldqpb}{2})/\hbar} \elemm{\boldqb+\frac{\boldqpb}{2},\boldqs}{\hateqrdm}{\boldqb-\frac{\boldqpb}{2},\boldqps} e^{-i\boldpb \cdot (\boldqb-\frac{\boldqpb}{2})/\hbar}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} and by noticing that $f(\hatqbz)\ket{\varqbz' = f(\varqbz')\ket{\varqbz' $ for any function $f$, then one can write \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \ket{\boldqb-\frac{\boldqpb}{2}} e^{-i\boldpb \cdot (\boldqb-\frac{\boldqpb}{2})/\hbar} = e^{-i\boldpb \cdot \hatboldqb/\hbar} \ket{\boldqb-\frac{\boldqpb}{2}} , \end{aligned}\end{equation} such that we can rewrite the argument of the integral in Eq.\eqref{eq:def_wigner_2 as \begin{equation}\label{eq:wigner_3} \begin{aligned} e^{i\boldpb \cdot (\boldqb+\frac{\boldqpb}{2})/\hbar} \elemm{\boldqb+\frac{\boldqpb}{2},\boldqs}{\hateqrdm}{\boldqb-\frac{\boldqpb}{2},\boldqps} e^{-i\boldpb \cdot (\boldqb-\frac{\boldqpb}{2})/\hbar} = \elemm{\boldqb+\frac{\boldqpb}{2},\boldqs}{ e^{i\boldpb \cdot \hatboldqb /\hbar} \hateqrdm e^{-i\boldpb \cdot \hatboldqb/\hbar} }{\boldqb-\frac{\boldqpb}{2},\boldqps}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} We then see that the argument of the integral in Eq. \eqref{eq:def_wigner_2 is nothing but the off-diagonal matrix element of the similarity transformation of $\hateqrdm$ by the operator $\exp({-i\boldpb \cdot \hatboldqb/\hbar )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-159: Instead of performing the similarity transformation directly on $\exp(-\beta \fulh)$, we use \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \hat{U} e^{\hat{A}} \hat{U}^{-1} = e^{\hat{U}\hat{A}\hat{U}^{-1}}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} and first perform the similarity transformation of $\fulh$ and then take the exponential of the similarity transformed Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-160: On has then to compute \begin{equation}\label{eq:h_similarity_1} \begin{aligned} \wignerh = e^{i\boldpb \cdot \hatboldqb /\hbar} \fulh e^{-i\boldpb \cdot \hatboldqb/\hbar}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} which is an operator explicitly depending on the parameter $\boldpb$.
elements.sent-2411-161: We use the then the Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff relation \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \wignerh = \fulh +i \frac{\boldpb }{\hbar} \big[ \hatboldqb, \fulh \big] + \frac{1}{2} \big(i \frac{\boldpb }{\hbar}\big)^2 \big[ \hatboldqb, \big[ \hatboldqb, \fulh \big] \big] + \hdots \end{aligned}\end{equation} which is essentially an infinite series of nested commutator of $\hatboldqb$ with $\big[ \hatboldqb, \fulh \big]$.
elements.sent-2411-162: As $\big[\hatboldqb,\hathqm\big]=0$, the first commutator yields \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \big[ \hatboldqb, \fulh \big] = \big[ \hatboldqb, \frac{\hatboldpb^2}{2M} \big] = \frac{i \hbar}{M} \hatboldpb. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Then the second commutator reads \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \big[ \hatboldqb, \big[ \hatboldqb, \fulh \big] \big] = \frac{i \hbar}{M}\big[ \hatboldqb, \hatboldpb\big] = -\frac{\hbar^2}{M}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} As $\big[ \hatboldqb, \big[ \hatboldqb, \fulh \big] \big]$ yields a constant, all higher order nested commutators are zero such that higher order terms vanish.
elements.sent-2411-163: Therefore, the similarity transformed Hamiltonian of Eq. \eqref{eq:h_similarity_1 yields \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \wignerh = \fulh +i \frac{\boldpb }{\hbar} \big[ \hatboldqb, \fulh \big] + \frac{1}{2} \big(i \frac{\boldpb }{\hbar}\big)^2 \big[ \hatboldqb, \big[ \hatboldqb, \fulh \big] \big] = \fulh -\frac{\boldpb }{M} \hatboldpb + \frac{\boldpb^2 }{2 M}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} This is essentially the same result that Wigner obtained in equation 16 of his original paper\cite{Wigner-PR-32 but in the case of a mixed quantum-classical system.}}
elements.sent-2411-164: We note in passing that if we perform the Wigner transformation on $\fulh$ we obtain \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \wignerh(\boldqb,\boldpb) = \int d\boldqpb \elemm{\boldqb+\frac{\boldqpb}{2},\boldqs}{\wignerh}{\boldqb-\frac{\boldqpb}{2},\boldqps} \end{aligned}\end{equation} but as \begin{equation}\elemm{\boldqb+\frac{\boldqpb}{2},\boldqs}{ \hatboldpb^n}{\boldqb-\frac{\boldqpb}{2}} = (-i\hbar)^n\delta(\boldqpb)^{(n)},\end{equation} where $\delta(\boldqpb)^{(n)}$ is the $n$-th order derivative of the delta distribution, we then obtain \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \wignerh(\boldqb,\boldpb) = \hathqm + \boldvextmm(\boldqb) + \boldwmm(\boldqb) + \frac{\boldpb^2 }{2 M}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Therefore as expected, the partial Wigner transformation on the $(\boldqb,\boldqpb)$ variables of the quantum Hamiltonian is the classical function associated with the $(\boldqb,\boldpb)$ variables.

abla_{ oldqb}  ig\} ig). \end{aligned}\end{equation} We would like now to take a limit such that we arrive at the mixed QM-MM system: all the $
qm$ "light" particles must be described fully quantum (i.e. with the Laplacian operator as the kinetic energy), while the $
mm$ "heavy" particles must be treated classically (i.e. with the classical kinetic energy).
elements.sent-2411-166: In other words, we would like to find a limit such that the last two terms of the right-hand side of Eq. \eqref{eq:h_similarity_2 vanishes.}
elements.sent-2411-167: We cannot simply take either $\hbar \rightarrow 0$ or $\alpha \rightarrow 0$ as we would loose either the kinetic energy of the light particles or heavy particles, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-168: A way to fulfill these requirements is to let simultaneously $\hbar \rightarrow 0$ and $m\rightarrow 0$ such that the ratio $\zeta \equiv \hbar^2/m $ is unchanged, while $M$ remains constant.
elements.sent-2411-169: Then the limit we want to take is the following \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim \limits_{\substack{ \hbar \to 0 m \to 0}} \frac{\hbar^2}{m} = \zeta \Leftrightarrow m \underset{\hbar \rightarrow 0}{\sim} \frac{1}{\zeta} \hbar^2, \end{aligned}\end{equation} or said in other words, $m$ must tend towards zero typically as fast as $\hbar^2$.
elements.sent-2411-170: We can also determine how $\alpha$ behaves when $\hbar\rightarrow 0$ \begin{equation}\alpha \underset{\hbar \rightarrow 0}{\sim} \frac{1}{M \zeta} \hbar^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-171: The behaviour of $\wignerh$ when $\hbar\rightarrow 0$ is then \begin{equation}\label{eq:h_similarity_3} \begin{aligned} \wignerh \underset{\hbar \rightarrow 0}{\sim} \hatboldwqm + \hatboldwmm + \hatboldwqmmm + \hatboldvextqm + \hatboldvextmm - \frac{\zeta}{2} \Delta_{\boldqs} + \frac{\boldpb^2 }{2M} + \frac{1}{M} \big\{ {i \boldpb\hbar }\nabla_{\boldqb} - \hbar^2\frac{1}{2} \Delta_{\boldqb} \big\}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} such that we can write the operator $\wignerh$ in terms of power of $\hbar$ when $\hbar \rightarrow 0$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:h_similarity_5} \begin{aligned} \wignerh \underset{\hbar \rightarrow 0}{\sim} \wignerhz + \hbar \wignerhone + \hbar^2 \wignerhtwo, \end{aligned}\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\label{eq:h_similarity_6} \begin{aligned} \wignerhz = - \frac{\hatboldps^2}{2m} + \hatboldwqm + \hatboldwqmmm + \hatboldvextqm + \frac{\boldpb^2 }{2M} + \hatboldwmm + \hatboldvextmm , \end{aligned}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:h_similarity_7} \begin{aligned} \wignerhone = \frac{i\boldpb}{M} \nabla_{\boldqb} , \end{aligned}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:h_similarity_8} \begin{aligned} \wignerhtwo = -\frac{1}{2M} \Delta_{\boldqb}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} We note that this is different from the result of Wigner as in his paper he performed a semi-classical expansion purely in terms of the $\hbar$ constant, whenever here we also involve the ratio of the masses $\alpha$ in order to differentiate the two distinct types of particles.

elements.subsubsection-2411-007: Rewriting $\ecano[\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm]$ as an average value on probability measures
elements.sent-2411-184: We use the adiabatic basis ${\basisq = \{\ket{\Psi_i(\boldqb) ,0\le i\le \infty\}$ to develop the quantum or mixed quantum-classical operators.}}
elements.sent-2411-185: More precisely, $\basisq$ consists in the complete set of eigenvectors of the following Schrodinger equation \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \big( \hathtotqm + \hatboldwqmmm(\boldqb)\big)\ket{\Psi_i(\boldqb)} = E_i(\boldqb) \ket{\Psi_i(\boldqb)}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} such that the Hamiltonian $\hathqm + \hatboldwqmmm(\boldqb)$ is naturally written as \begin{equation}\label{eq:def_adiab} \hathtotqm + \hatboldwqmmm(\boldqb) = \sum_i E_i(\boldqb)\ket{\Psi_i(\boldqb)}\bra{\Psi_i(\boldqb)},\end{equation} and the operator $\hatrdmqm(\boldqb)$ is written as follows \begin{equation}\hatrdmqm(\boldqb) = \sum_i \rdmqm_{ii}(\boldqb)\ket{\Psi_i(\boldqb)}\bra{\Psi_i(\boldqb)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-186: Because of the properties of density matrices, the real-numbers $\rdmqm_{ii (\boldqb)$ fulfill \begin{equation}\label{eq:prop_dens} \rdmqm_{ii}(\boldqb) \ge 0, \sum_{i \in \basisq} \rdmqm_{ii}(\boldqb) = 1.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-187: Therefore, as $\rdmmm(\boldqb)$ is a probability distribution and because of the properties of Eq. \eqref{eq:prop_dens , if we define the function $\mu(\boldqb,i)$ as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} {\mathbb R}^{3\nqm}\times {\mathbb N} \rightarrow {\mathbb R}, (\boldqb,i)\mapsto \mu(\boldqb,i) = \rdmmm(\boldqb)\rdmqm_{ii}(\boldqb), \end{aligned}\end{equation} then $\mu(\boldqb,i)$ fulfils the requirements for a probability measure, i.e. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mu(\boldqb,i) \ge 0\,\,\forall\,\, (\boldqb,i), \quad \intdqb \sum_{i}\mu(\boldqb,i)=1. \end{aligned}\end{equation} For the sake of compactness, we define $\bigx=(\boldqb,i)$, such that we can rewrite the probability measure as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mu(\boldqb,i) \equiv \mu(\bigx), \quad \intdqb \sum_{i} \equiv \intbigx. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The equilibrium density $\hateqrdm$ of Eq. \eqref{eq:eq_qmmm_rho can be written using the adiabatic basis of Eq. \hatboldwqmmm(\boldqb))}e^{-\beta\hathtotmm(\boldqb)} = \frac{1}{\eqz} \sum_i e^{-\beta\epsilon(\boldqb,i)} \ket{\Psi_i(\boldqb)} \bra{\Psi_i(\boldqb)}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} which corresponds to the measure $\mux=\mueqx $ defined as follows \begin{equation}\mueqx \equiv \frac{e^{-\beta\epsilon(\bigx)}}{\eqz},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \epsilon(\bigx) = \epsilon(\boldqb,i) \equiv E_i(\boldqb) + \hathtotmm(\boldqb) . \end{aligned}\end{equation} We introduce the notation for an average value over a probability measure $\mux$ as \begin{equation}\intbigx \mux f(\bigx) = \langle f \rangle_\mu,\end{equation} such that the functional $\ecano[\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm]$ can be rewritten as an average value on the measure $\mu(\bigx)$ \begin{equation}\label{eq:ecano_mu} \begin{aligned} \ecano[\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm] = \tr\big\{\hatrdmqm\rdmmm\big( \hathtotmm + \hathqm + \hatboldwqmmm + \frac{1}{\beta}\log(\hatrdmqm\rdmmm)\big)\} = \intdqb \sum_{i }\mu(\boldqb,i)\big( \epsilon(\boldqb,i) + \frac{1}{\beta}\log(\mu(\boldqb,i)) = \intbigx\mu(\bigx)\big( \epsilon(\bigx) + \frac{1}{\beta}\log(\mu(\bigx)) = -\frac{1}{\beta}\intbigx\mu(\bigx)\log(\frac{e^{-\beta\epsilon(\bigx)}}{\mu(\bigx)}) = -\frac{1}{\beta}\langle \log(\frac{e^{-\beta\epsilon}}{\mu}) \rangle_{\mu} \equiv \ecano[\mu] . \begin{aligned} \ecano[\mueq] = -\frac{1}{\beta} \log(\eqz), \end{aligned}\end{equation} which is of course the correct Helmholtz free energy.}}}}

elements.subsubsection-2411-008: Use of Jensen inequality to prove variational character
elements.sent-2411-189: As the function $-\log(x)$ is strictly convex, we will now use Jensen's inequality from convex analysis and probability measures to prove that $\ecano[\mu]$ is bounded from below by the Helmholtz free energy and that $\mueq$ is the only minimizer.
elements.sent-2411-190: The Jensen's inequality can be stated as follows.
elements.sent-2411-191: If $\varphi$ is a convex function (i.e. always above its tangent lines), $\mu(\bigx)$ a probability measure on $\bigx \in E$ where $E$ is a normed vector space, $g(\bigx)$ a function from $E$ to ${\mathbb R}$, then Jensen's equality states that \begin{equation}\label{eq:jensen_av} \varphi(\langle g \rangle_{\mu}) \le \langle \varphi(g)\rangle_{\mu},\end{equation} or written in terms of integrals \begin{equation}\label{eq:jensen_int} \varphi\bigg( \int \text{d}\bigx \mu(\bigx) g(\bigx)\bigg) \le \int \text{d}\bigx \mu(\bigx) \varphi\big(g(\bigx)\big).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-192: As $-\log(x)$ is convex, the Jensen's equality can be applied to $\ecano[\mu]$ of Eq. \eqref{eq:ecano_mu by setting \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} g(\bigx) = \frac{e^{-\beta\epsilon(\bigx)}}{\mux}, \varphi(x) = -\frac{1}{\beta} \log(x). \end{aligned}\end{equation} Therefore the inequality of Eq. \eqref{eq:jensen_av becomes \begin{equation}\label{eq:jensen_f} -\frac{1}{\beta} \log\langle \frac{e^{-\beta\epsilon}}{\mu} \rangle_{\mu} \le -\frac{1}{\beta}\langle \log(\frac{e^{-\beta\epsilon}}{\mu}) \rangle_{\mu},\end{equation} and as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \langle \frac{e^{-\beta\epsilon}}{\mu} \rangle_{\mu} = \intbigx \mux \frac{e^{-\beta\epsilon(x))}}{\mux} = \eqz, \end{aligned}\end{equation} one can rewrite Eq. \eqref{eq:jensen_f as \begin{equation}\label{eq:jensen_f2} \begin{aligned} -\frac{1}{\beta} \log(\eqz) \le \ecano[\mu], \end{aligned}\end{equation} which guarantees the variational character.}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: Eventually, the strict convexity of the $-\log(x)$ function implies that the Jensen's inequality becomes an equality if and only if $g(\bigx)=cst$, which translates into \begin{equation}g(\bigx)=cst \Leftrightarrow \frac{e^{-\beta\epsilon(\bigx)}}{\mux} = cst \Leftrightarrow \mux \propto e^{-\beta\epsilon(\bigx)},\end{equation} therefore implying that $\mueqx$ is the only minimizer.

elements.sent-2411-198: Therefore it is worth splitting the ED in terms of $\boldqb$ and $\boldpb$ dependence \begin{equation}\label{eq:eq_qmmm_rho_p_2} \begin{aligned} \mathcal{M}(\boldqb,\boldpb,\boldqs,\boldqps) = \frac{e^{-\beta \boldtb}}{z_{\boldpb}} \eqrdm(\boldqb,\boldqs,\boldqps), \end{aligned}\end{equation} with {$\boldtb$ being the purely classical kinetic energy defined in Eq. \eqref{eq:def_t_cl , and \begin{equation}\label{eq:eq_qmmm_rho} \begin{aligned} \eqrdm(\boldqb,\boldqs,\boldqps) = \frac{\elemm{\boldqs}{e^{-\beta(\hathqm + \hatboldwqmmm(\boldqb))}}{\boldqps}e^{-\beta\vmm(\boldqb)}}{\eqz}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:vtotmm} \begin{aligned} \vmm = \boldvextmm + \boldwmm, \end{aligned}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:eq_qmmm_z_p2} \begin{aligned} z_{\boldpb} = \int\dpb e^{-\beta \boldtb}= \bigg(\frac{2\pi M}{\beta} \bigg)^{\frac{3}{2}\nmm }, \end{aligned}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:eq_qmmm_z} \begin{aligned} \eqz = \intdqb \intdqs \elemm{\boldqs}{e^{-\beta(\hathqm + \hatboldwqmmm(\boldqb))}}{\boldqs}e^{-\beta\vmm(\boldqb)}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:eq_qmmm_z_tot} \begin{aligned} \eqztot = z_{\boldpb}\eqz , \end{aligned}\end{equation} such that the free energy can be written as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \potcanotot = \potcanoz+F_{\text{id}} , \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq:Fid} F_{\text{id}} = -\frac{\log(z_{\boldpb})}{\beta},\end{equation} is the Helmotz free energy of an ideal classical fluid without external potential, and \begin{equation}\label{eq:def_pot_cano_int} \potcanoz = -\frac{\log(\eqz)}{\beta}\end{equation} is the interacting part of the Helmholtz free energy of the $\qmb$/$\mmb$ system.}}
elements.sent-2411-199: Secondly, the object $\eqrdm(\boldqb,\boldqs,\boldqps)$ in Eq.\eqref{eq:eq_qmmm_rho can be expressed as the product of a purely quantum density matrix { $\eqrdmqm(\boldqs,\boldqps,\boldqb)$ (more precisely its matrix element evaluated in real space) and a purely classical function$ \eqrdmmm(\boldqb,\boldpb)$},i.e. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \label{eq:Peq=f*rho} \eqrdm(\boldqb,\boldpb,\boldqs,\boldqps) = \eqrdmqm(\boldqs,\boldqps,\boldqb) \eqrdmmm(\boldqb,\boldpb), \end{aligned}\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\label{eq:eq_rdmqm} \begin{aligned} \eqrdmqm(\boldqs,\boldqps,\boldqb) =\elemm{\boldqs}{\hateqrdmqm(\boldqb)}{\boldqps}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:eq_rdmqm_2} \begin{aligned} \hateqrdmqm(\boldqb) = \frac{1}{\eqzqm(\boldqb)}e^{-\beta(\hathqm + \hatboldwqmmm(\boldqb))}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:eq_qm_z} \begin{aligned} \eqzqm(\boldqb) = \intdqs \elemm{\boldqs}{e^{-\beta(\hathqm + \hatboldwqmmm(\boldqb))}}{\boldqs}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:eq_rdmmm} \begin{aligned} \eqrdmmm(\boldqb) = \frac{\eqzqm(\boldqb)}{\eqz}e^{-\beta\vmm(\boldqb)}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} $\eqz$ and $\eqzqm(\boldqb)$ are related through the following equation \begin{equation}\label{eq:eq_qmmm_z_bis} \begin{aligned} \eqz = \intdqb \eqzqm(\boldqb)e^{-\beta\vmm(\boldqb)}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} {It is noteworthy that $\hateqrdmqm(\boldqb)$ defined in Eq. \eqref{eq:eq_rdmqm ,\eqref{eq:eq_rdmqm_2 is a positive definite operator and that $\eqrdmmm(\boldqb)$ of Eq. \eqref{eq:eq_rdmmm is also positive definite and normalized to unity.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-200: Because $\rdmmm$ does not depend on the quantum variables and that we chose the normalization condition of Eq. \eqref{eq:norm_cond , we can naturally decompose the entropy as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \entrop[\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm] = S_{\qm}^{\mm}[\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm] + S_{\mm}[\rdmmm], \end{aligned}\end{equation} with $S_{\mm [\rdmmm]$ being the purely classical dimension-less entropy \begin{equation}\label{eq:s_mm} \begin{aligned} S_{\mm}[\rdmmm] = -\intdqb\,\rdmmm(\boldqb) \log(\rdmmm(\boldqb)), \end{aligned}\end{equation} and $S_{\qm ^{\mm [\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm] $ is the quantum-classical dimension-less entropy \begin{equation}\label{eq:s_qmmm} \begin{aligned} S_{\qm}^{\mm}[\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm] = -\tr\big\{\hatrdmqm\rdmmm\log(\hatrdmqm)\big\} = -\intdqb\,\rdmmm(\boldqb)\intdqs\,\rdmqm(\boldqs,\boldqs,\boldqb) \log(\rdmqm(\boldqs,\boldqs,\boldqb)), \end{aligned}\end{equation} which depends explicitly on both $\hatrdmqm$ and $\rdmmm$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-201: We can similarly decompose $\uint$ as follows \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \uint[\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm] = E_{\mm}[\rdmmm] + E_\mm^{\text{ext}}[\rdmmm] + E_\qm[\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm] +E_\qm^{\text{ext}}[\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm] + E_{\qm}^{\mm}[\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm], \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $E_\mm[\rdmmm]$ is the mutual interaction of the classical part \begin{equation}\label{eq:def_emm} \begin{aligned} E_\mm[\rdmmm] =\intdqb \rdmmm(\boldqb) \boldwmm(\boldqb), \end{aligned}\end{equation} $E_\mm^{\text{ext [\rdmmm]$ is the classical part of the functional associated to the external potential \begin{equation}\label{eq:def_vmmext} \begin{aligned} E_\mm^{\text{ext}}[\rdmmm] =\intdqb \rdmmm(\boldqb) \boldvextmm(\boldqb), \end{aligned}\end{equation} $E_\qm[\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm]$ is the kinetic and mutual interaction of the quantum part \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} E_\qm[\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm] =\tr\{(\hatboldts + \hatboldwqm) \rdmmm\hatrdmqm\} =\intdqb \rdmmm(\boldqb) \quad \intdqs \dqps (\bf{t}(\boldqs,\boldqps) + \wqm(\boldqs,\boldqps))\rdmqm(\boldqs,\boldqps,\boldqb) , \end{aligned}\end{equation} $E_\qm^{\text{ext [\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm]$ is the quantum part of the functional associated to the external potential \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} E_\qm^{\text{ext}}[\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm] =\tr\{\hatboldvextqm \rdmmm\hatrdmqm\} =\intdqb \rdmmm(\boldqb) \intdqs \boldvextqm (\boldqs,\boldqs) \rdmqm(\boldqs,\boldqs,\boldqb), \end{aligned}\end{equation} and eventually $ E_{\qm ^{\mm [\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm]$ is the quantum-classical interaction, \begin{equation}\label{eq:e_qmmm} \begin{aligned} E_{\qm}^{\mm}[\hatrdmqm,\rdmmm] = \tr\{\hatboldwqmmm \rdmmm \hatrdmqm\} = \intdqb \rdmmm(\boldqb) \intdqs \boldwqmmm(\boldqb,\boldqs)\rdmqm(\boldqs,\boldqs,\boldqb). \end{aligned}\end{equation} It is noteworthy here to highlight that the form of Eq. \eqref{eq:e_qmmm is quite general as it consists in a local potential $\boldwqmmm(\boldqb,\boldqs)$.}}}}}}}

elements.section-2411-009: Recall of the variational formulation of statistical quantum mechanics in the canonical ensemble
elements.sent-2411-340: Just as the ground state energy and wave function is often formalized in terms of the variational principle, there exists a similar functional formulation of quantum statistical mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-341: As it might not be widely spread in the quantum chemistry community, which consists in the main audience of this paper, we recall here this variational formulation.
elements.sent-2411-342: In the canonical ensemble, the equilibrium density (ED) matrix $\rdmneq$ has the following expression \begin{equation}\label{eq:def_ed_cano} \rdmneq = \frac{e^{-\beta \fulhn}}{\zfunc},\end{equation} with $\beta^{-1 = k_B T$ and $\zfunc$ is the partition function \begin{equation}\zfunc = \trn\{ e^{-\beta \fulhn} \},\end{equation} where we introduced the trace of an $N$-electron operator, $\hat{O ^N$, which is given in real space by \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \trn\{ \hat{O}^N \} = \int \text{d}q_1 \hdots \text{d}q_{N} \elemm{\rlots{N}}{\hat{O}}{\rlots{N}} = \int \text{d}q_1 \hdots \text{d}q_{N} \oprr. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The corresponding Helmholtz free energy is then obtained by \begin{equation}\label{eq:def_pot_cano} \potcanoz = - \frac{1}{\beta} \log(\zfunc).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-343: In the latter framework, $\potcanoz$ can be expressed as the minimum of a free-energy density matrix functional \begin{equation}\label{eq:pot_cano_1} \potcanoz = \min_{\rdmn} \potcano[\rdmn],\end{equation} where $\rdmn$ are $N$-representable density matrices, i.e. density matrices representing statistical mixing of $N$-particles density matrices.

elements.sent-2411-542: With these definitions, the external part of the grand potential $\omega_\text{ext}$, in Eq.~\eqref{eq:Omega_ext , becomes \begin{equation}\omega_\text{ext} [\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC}] = \left( \vextqm \mid \densqm^{\GC} \right) + \left( v_{\text{ext}}^{{\mm}} \mid \densmm^{\GC} \right),\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\left( v_{\text{ext}}^{{\mm}} \mid \densmm^{\GC} \right) \equiv \int dQ \densmm^{\GC} (Q ) \, v_{\text{ext}}^{{\mm}}(Q) \, ,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\left( \vextqm \mid \densqm^{\GC} \right) \equiv \int \densqm^{\GC}(q) \, \vextqm(q).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-543: We can then rewrite the minimization of Eq. \eqref{eq:Var_prin_GC1 using the Levy-Lieb constrained search \begin{equation}\Omega_0 = \min_{(\densqm^{\GC},\densmm^{\GC})}\quad\min_{\dmgc \rightarrow(\densqm^{\GC},\densmm^{\GC}) } \Omega[ \dmgc ],\end{equation} and by introducing the Levy-Lieb QM/MM universal functional $\Omega_\text{int [\densqm^{\GC , \densmm^{\GC ]$ in the semi-grand-canonical ensemble as \begin{equation}\label{eq:Omega_LL1} \Omega_\text{int}[\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC}] = \min_{\dmgc \rightarrow (\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC})} \omega_\text{int}[\dmgc],\end{equation} we can then rewrite the grand canonical potential as a minimization over only the one-body QM and MM densities \begin{equation}\Omega_0 = \min_{(\densqm^{\GC},\densmm^{\GC})} \big\{ \Omega_\text{int}[\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC}] + \left( \vextqm \mid \densqm^{\GC} \right) + \left( v_{\text{ext}}^{{\mm}}\mid \densmm^{\GC} \right) \big\}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-544: Although the exact expression of this functional is unknown, as in the case of the canonical ensemble, we choose to express it using known universal functional for the purely quantum and classical components.
elements.sent-2411-545: Based on Eq.~\eqref{eq:Omega_int , we obtain \begin{equation}\label{eq:Omega_LL2} \Omega_\text{int}[\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC} ] = \fqm[\densqm^{\GC}] + \Omega_\mm [\densmm^{\GC}] + \Omega_{\qm}^\mm[\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC}],\end{equation} where $\fqm[\densqm^{\GC ]$ is the universal purely quantum one-body density functional, \begin{equation}\label{eq:mermin} \fqm[\densqm^{\GC}] = \min_{\hatrdmqm\rightarrow\densqm^{\GC} } \text{Tr}\{\hatrdmqm\big[ \hatboldts + \hatboldwqm + \kb T \log(\hatrdmqm)\big]\},\end{equation} and $\Omega_\mm[\densmm^{\GC ] $ is the universal classical one-body functional of Evans\cite{Evans-AP-79 , but expressed using the constrained search formalism of Levy-Lieb.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-546: Lastly, in Eq.~\eqref{eq:Omega_LL2 , we define the term $\Omega_\qm^\mm$, which represents the interactions between the QM and the MM parts \begin{equation}\Omega_{\qm}^\mm [\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC}] = \Omega_\text{int}[\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC}] - \fqm [\densqm^{\GC}] - \Omega_\mm[\densmm^{\GC}].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-547: In the case of a two-body QM/MM interaction, we can then decompose this complex term into mean-field and correlation parts, namely \begin{equation}\label{eq:Omega_qmmm[rhomel,rhomm]} \Omega_{\qm}^\mm [\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC}] = \varepsilon_{\qm}^\mm [\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC}] + \delta \Omega_{\qm}^\mm [\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC}],\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\label{eq:eps_qmmmGC[rhomel,rhomm]} \varepsilon_{\qm}^\mm [\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC}] = \iint \densmm^{\GC}(Q ) \, v_\qm^\mm(Q,q) \, \densqm^{\GC}(q) \, \mathrm{d} Q \, dq,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:dOmega_qmmm[rhomel,rhomm]} \delta \Omega_{\qm}^\mm [\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC}] = \Omega_\text{int}[\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC}] - \fqm[\densqm^{\GC}] - E_\mm[\densmm^{\GC}] - \varepsilon_{\qm}^\mm [\densqm^{\GC}, \densmm^{\GC}],\end{equation} where $\varepsilon_{\qm ^\mm [\densqm^{\GC , \densmm^{\GC ]$ is the mean-field interaction term directly computable, while $\delta \Omega_{\qm ^\mm [\densqm^{\GC , \densmm^{\GC ]$ is the unknown term containing QM/MM correlation.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-548: We emphasize that Eq.~\eqref{eq:Omega_LL1 constitutes, to our knowledge, the first exact formulation of a Levy-Lieb universal functional for a QM/MM system in the semi-grand-canonical ensemble.}
